title: Chapter 18: The Call Back
content: Chloe called Jake at 10:00 PM.
She did it because delaying felt too much like hiding. And she was done hiding.
He picked up on the first ring.
"Chloe?" His voice was eager. Hopeful. The voice of a man who thought this was the beginning of a reconciliation.
"Hey, Jake."
"I'm so glad you called. I was worried you wouldn't. I know things are... complicated."

"Yeah. Complicated." She was sitting on Elena's couch, bare feet on the coffee table, a notebook open in her lap. She had written three questions she needed answered. She would not waste time on anything else.
"I want to see you," he said. "Can we meet?"
"Where?"
"There's a coffee shop on Fourth Street. The one we used to go to. Remember?"

She remembered. Two years ago, when things were still good. Before he started talking about her "potential" and how she was "wasting it." Before he started suggesting she take the medication her father recommended.
"Tomorrow. 2:00 PM."
"Great! I'll be there. I can't wait to — "
She hung up.
Let him wonder whether the last part was deliberate.
The next morning, Chloe went to Mia's new apartment.

It was a studio in a converted warehouse — exposed brick, high ceilings, a window that overlooked the train tracks. Mia had already set up her workstation: three monitors, a server tower under the desk, and a whiteboard covered in network diagrams.
"You've been busy," Chloe said.
"I had to rebuild my infrastructure. The FBI confiscated my old equipment." Mia did not look up from her keyboard. "So far, I'm getting better results with the new setup."
"I need a favor."
"Always."
"Jake. I'm meeting him today. I need to know what he's hiding."
Mia finally looked up. "You think he's hiding something?"
"He called me yesterday. Said he didn't know what he was signing when he committed me. Said my father tricked him."
"And you don't believe him?"
"Jake is not stupid. He's a corporate lawyer. He doesn't sign anything without reading it. If he signed the commitment papers, he knew exactly what they were."
Mia turned to her keyboard. "Full background check?"
"Financials, communications, anything that connects him to my father's firm."
"Give me two hours."
Chloe checked her phone. One forty-five. The meeting was at two.
"Make it one."
The coffee shop on Fourth Street had not changed in two years.

Same exposed brick. Same mismatched furniture. Same chalkboard menu with the cute hand-drawn latte art. Chloe had spent dozens of afternoons here during her relationship with Jake, pretending that everything was fine.
He was already there when she arrived.
He stood up when he saw her. Smiled. That smile she had once found charming — the one that now looked rehearsed.
"Chloe." He opened his arms.
She did not hug him. She sat down across the table.
"Hey, Jake."
He lowered his arms. The smile flickered but held. "I ordered your usual. I hope that's okay."
A latte appeared in front of her. The barista set it down with a cheerful "Enjoy!" Chloe looked at the cup. Two years ago, this would have made her feel seen. Now it felt like a prop.
"Thanks."
"So," Jake said, sitting down. "How are you? Really?"
"I've been better. I've been worse."

"I can't imagine what you went through. When I saw the news — " He shook his head. "I felt sick. I had no idea that place was like that. Your father told me it was a wellness retreat. A place where you could get help."
"My father told you a lot of things."
"I should have asked more questions. I know that now. I just... I trusted him. He's your dad. I thought he wanted what was best for you."
Chloe picked up the latte. Took a sip. It was exactly how she used to like it — oat milk, extra shot, no sugar. She had changed her coffee order a year ago. He did not know that.
"Tell me about the day you signed the papers," she said.
Jake's expression shifted. A micro-flinch. Almost invisible.
"It was... a normal day. Your father called me, said he was worried about you. Said you had been acting erratically, that you refused to see a doctor. He asked me to come to his office."
"What time?"
"What?"
"What time of day?"
"I don't remember. Afternoon, maybe."
"You're a lawyer, Jake. You bill in six-minute increments. You remember what time you went anywhere."
He was quiet.
Chloe set the latte down. She had not touched her notebook. She had not needed to. The questions were already in her head.

"Let me tell you what I think happened," she said. "My father called you. He told you I was becoming a problem. He said the family had a solution — a facility that could help me 'reset.' But he needed a family member to sign the admission forms, and he didn't want his own name on the paperwork."
Jake's face was carefully blank.
"You said yes. Because you still thought we might get back together. Or because he offered you something. I haven't figured out which yet."
"Chloe, that's not — "
"I'm not finished."
He stopped.

"You went to his office. You signed three documents: a medical consent form, a financial agreement, and a non-disclosure agreement. The NDA was the key — it meant I could never talk about what happened inside. You signed it without reading it. Or you read it and signed it anyway."
"That's — "
"Which one was it?" she asked. "Did you know what you were signing, or did you trust my father enough to sign anything he put in front of you?"
He did not answer.
Chloe nodded slowly. "That's what I thought."
She pulled an envelope out of her bag and slid it across the table.
"What is this?"

"A subpoena. You're going to testify in the civil case against Serenity Peak and Hart, Chen and Associates. You're going to tell the court exactly what my father said to you when he asked you to sign those papers."
Jake stared at the envelope. His face had lost its practiced warmth. "You can't make me testify against your father."
"I'm not making you testify against anyone. I'm making you tell the truth. If the truth happens to implicate my father, that's a consequence of his actions, not my manipulation."
"This is insane."
"This is consequences." Chloe stood up. "You had a choice, Jake. You chose to help lock me up. Now you get to live with what that means."
She picked up the latte — still untouched — and dropped it in the trash on her way out.
Mia was waiting outside in Elena's car.
"How did it go?" she asked as Chloe got in.
"He's going to testify. He doesn't know it yet, but he will."
"I found something." Mia turned her laptop toward Chloe. "Jake's been getting payments from Hart, Chen and Associates for the last eight months. Monthly retainer. Fifteen thousand dollars."
Chloe read the screen. Bank statements. Wire transfers. The pattern was clear.
"My father was paying him," she said. "Even before I was committed."
"Before. During. After. The payments stopped the day your story broke."
Chloe stared at the screen. The betrayal was not just personal. It was financial. Contractual. Her father had bought Jake's loyalty — and Jake had sold it.
"Can you prove he knew what he was signing?"

"Already found it. Email chain between your father and Jake, dated three days before your admission. Your father attached the facility brochure and the consent forms. Jake replied: 'Looks good. See you Tuesday.'"
Chloe closed the laptop. "He knew."
"He knew."
She looked out the window. The coffee shop was shrinking in the side mirror. Jake was probably still sitting at the table, staring at the subpoena, realizing his life had just changed.
"Good," she said.
That night, Chloe sat on Elena's rooftop with a notebook and a bottle of water.
The city spread out below her. Lights. Traffic. The distant hum of a thousand lives being lived simultaneously.

She had been free for three days. In three days, she had confronted the man who locked her up, dismantled the facility that held her, and started building a case that would take down her father's firm.
Not bad.
She opened her notebook and wrote:

*Phase 1: Exit — Complete.*
*Phase 2: Exposure — In progress.*
*Phase 3: Takeover — Planning.*
The next step was the facility itself. The parent company would sell it at a discount now that the scandal had destroyed its value. She needed money. She needed a legal structure. She needed a plan.
She looked at the city lights and smiled.
*Give me a week,* she thought. *I'll have all three.*
